name: Fort Worth Independent School District
districtFact: Largest school district in Fort Worth and one of the 30 largest school districts in the United States, serving Tarrant County.

about.title: About the District
about.content: Fort Worth Independent School District serves approximately 80,000 students across 150 schools in Fort Worth and surrounding communities in Tarrant County, Texas.

planningTips: Fort Worth ISD ends the school year May 21 — about a week earlier than Dallas ISD and two weeks before Miami-Dade. Summer camps and childcare starting June 1 may leave a gap for Fort Worth families.

Fort Worth ISD and Dallas ISD are separate districts. If your family has children in both, confirm Spring Break and start dates on each district's calendar — they are usually close but not always identical.
Good Friday (March 26) is a school holiday in Fort Worth ISD. Families from states that don't observe Good Friday should mark this day off on their calendar — it falls one week after Spring Break.

ECHS students at Tarrant County College dual-enrollment schools may have additional orientation or testing days outside the standard calendar. Contact the campus directly for ECHS-specific scheduling.

districtFaqs.q: Is Fort Worth ISD the same as Dallas ISD?
districtFaqs.a: No. Fort Worth ISD and Dallas ISD are separate, independent school districts. Fort Worth ISD serves the city of Fort Worth and parts of Tarrant County. Dallas ISD serves the city of Dallas and parts of Dallas County. Despite being part of the same DFW metropolitan area, they have different school boards, different calendars, and different school start and end dates. Always check each district's official website for accurate dates.

districtFaqs.q: Is Fort Worth ISD connected to Texas Christian University (TCU)?
districtFaqs.a: No. Fort Worth Independent School District (Fort Worth ISD) is the K-12 public school system for the city of Fort Worth. Texas Christian University (TCU) is a separate private university in Fort Worth with its own semester academic calendar. TCU's schedule has no connection to Fort Worth ISD school dates.

districtFaqs.q: Why does Fort Worth ISD end the school year earlier than Dallas ISD?
districtFaqs.a: Fort Worth ISD's 2026-2027 school year ends May 21, compared to Dallas ISD's May 26 and Houston ISD's May 28. The difference reflects Fort Worth ISD's total instructional day count (175 days in 2026-2027) versus Dallas ISD's 180 days. Texas law sets a minimum of 180 instructional days for most districts, but waivers and program variations can result in slight differences between districts.

districtFaqs.q: Does Fort Worth ISD serve all addresses in the city of Fort Worth?
districtFaqs.a: No. Fort Worth ISD boundaries do not cover the entire city of Fort Worth. Several areas within Fort Worth city limits fall within neighboring independent school districts, including Eagle Mountain-Saginaw ISD, Keller ISD, White Settlement ISD, and others. Families should verify their assigned school district using their specific home address at fwisd.org — a Fort Worth mailing address does not guarantee Fort Worth ISD enrollment. This is especially important for families buying or renting in rapidly growing north and west Fort Worth areas.

districtFaqs.q: How does Fort Worth ISD compare with Eagle Mountain-Saginaw ISD or Keller ISD?
districtFaqs.a: Fort Worth ISD, Eagle Mountain-Saginaw ISD, and Keller ISD are separate, independent school districts serving different parts of Tarrant County. Fort Worth ISD is by far the largest, serving the urban core. Eagle Mountain-Saginaw and Keller ISD are suburban districts with their own academic calendars — spring break dates and first/last days may differ from Fort Worth ISD by a few days. Families buying in north Fort Worth suburbs should verify which specific district serves their address before finalizing enrollment.

districtFaqs.q: What is an Early College High School in Fort Worth ISD?
districtFaqs.a: Fort Worth ISD operates Early College High School (ECHS) programs in partnership with Tarrant County College. ECHS students take dual-enrollment college courses alongside their high school requirements, allowing them to earn up to 60 college credit hours — enough for an associate degree — by the time they graduate. ECHS students may have additional orientation days, testing schedules, or campus visits not reflected in the standard FWISD calendar. Contact the specific ECHS campus directly for program-specific scheduling requirements. Applications for ECHS programs typically open in fall for the following school year.

districtFaqs.q: Does Fort Worth ISD always observe Good Friday as a school holiday?
districtFaqs.a: Fort Worth ISD observes Good Friday as a school holiday. In 2026-2027, Good Friday falls on March 26 — one week after Spring Break ends on March 19. This creates a long Easter weekend after a regular school week. Good Friday observance is common in Texas school districts but is not universal nationwide — families relocating from states that treat Good Friday as a regular school day should mark March 26 on their calendar and arrange childcare if needed.

livingHere.intro: Fort Worth has a distinct identity from Dallas — rooted in cowboy heritage, Western culture, and a strong arts scene — despite sharing the DFW airport and metropolitan footprint. For families new to Tarrant County, a few local realities shape how the school calendar interacts with life in the city.

highlights.label: Fort Worth Stock Show, Rodeo, and School Life
highlights.detail: The Fort Worth Stock Show and Rodeo (typically late January through mid-February) is one of Texas's largest annual events and a regular FWISD field trip destination — agriculture, FFA (Future Farmers of America), and science classes attend during the show's three-week run. The Stockyards National Historic District sees peak tourism during this period and again at Spring Break; families planning local outings should expect higher crowds. On school days during Rodeo season, families driving near the Stockyards area should allow extra commute time on North Main Street and I-35W.

highlights.label: DFW Airport Proximity
highlights.detail: DFW International Airport sits between Fort Worth and Dallas, roughly 20 miles from most Fort Worth ISD schools. Spring Break departures from DFW fill quickly — Fort Worth ISD's March 15–19 break aligns with Dallas ISD, creating simultaneous peak demand at the airport.

highlights.label: NAS JRB Fort Worth and Military Family Enrollment
highlights.detail: Naval Air Station Joint Reserve Base Fort Worth (formerly Carswell AFB) is an active multi-branch reserve installation within Tarrant County. Military families stationed at NAS JRB are part of the Fort Worth ISD enrollment base, with PCS transfers arriving throughout the school year. Bring immunization records, prior school transcripts, and any IEP or 504 documentation when enrolling — FWISD can typically complete placement within a few business days. Military families departing on PCS orders should request a student transfer record packet from their campus before the move to speed enrollment at the receiving district.

highlights.label: Tornado Season
highlights.detail: Tarrant County sits in Tornado Alley, with peak severe weather risk March through May. Fort Worth ISD uses shelter-in-place protocols; late-afternoon storm systems can delay dismissal on short notice. Families should have a backup pickup plan for weather-delay days.

highlights.label: Bass Performance Hall and FWISD Arts Programs
highlights.detail: Bass Performance Hall in downtown Fort Worth is a major FWISD educational partner — the district has formal partnerships with the Fort Worth Symphony Orchestra and the Van Cliburn Foundation, resulting in subsidized student performances and curriculum-tied field trips throughout the school year. Elementary through high school students attend Bass Hall on school days as part of arts integration programs; permission slips for these trips are a regular occurrence for FWISD families. Students at the Fort Worth Academy of Fine Arts also use the Sundance Square corridor for performances tied to the school calendar.

highlights.label: Alliance Texas and North Corridor Growth
highlights.detail: Alliance Texas — one of the nation's largest inland ports — brings thousands of jobs to north Tarrant County in aviation, logistics, e-commerce, and manufacturing. Corporate relocations and hires for Alliance-area employers (including Amazon fulfillment, American Airlines Maintenance, and industrial manufacturers) drive consistent mid-year enrollment into north Fort Worth ISD schools. FWISD has opened new schools in the north corridor to keep pace with growth, and attendance boundaries in this area have been adjusted in recent years — families settling in north Fort Worth should verify their school assignment at fwisd.org using their specific address.

highlights.label: FWISD Signature Programs
highlights.detail: Fort Worth ISD operates distinctive programs not available in most neighboring districts. The Fort Worth Academy of Fine Arts (FWAFA) is a selective K-12 fine arts magnet serving the performing arts, visual arts, and creative writing — one of the few dedicated fine arts schools of its scale in North Texas. The district's Career and Technical Education (CTE) academies span 60+ industry pathways including aviation technology, healthcare, engineering, and culinary arts, with students earning industry certifications before graduation. Early College High School (ECHS) programs in partnership with Tarrant County College allow students to graduate with up to 60 free college credit hours. Applications for FWAFA and ECHS programs typically open in fall — families interested in specialty programs should contact FWISD's school choice office by October.

highlights.label: FWISD Annual Planning Rhythm
highlights.detail: Fort Worth ISD follows a distinct annual cycle that families benefit from mapping at the start of each school year. August 10: school begins — one of Texas's earliest start dates, with UIL athletic season launching simultaneously. January–February: Fort Worth Stock Show and Rodeo, with FWISD field trips running throughout the three-week event. March 15–19: Spring Break aligned with Dallas ISD. March 26: Good Friday school holiday, creating a long weekend one week after return from break. May 21: last day — a week earlier than Dallas ISD and two weeks before most national peers, making summer camp and childcare planning especially important. ECHS and CTE students follow an additional layer: fall Tarrant County College orientation, spring industry certification testing, and internship cycles that run parallel to the standard school calendar.
